q: Summarize the book of Romans: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written to the church at Rome (which he had not yet visited) from Corinth near the end of his third missionary journey, c. A.D. 57.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** The gospel as the power of God for salvation, unfolding how a righteous God justifies sinners by grace through faith in Christ, apart from works of the law, for both Jew and Gentile.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–3:20 — All humanity guilty under sin
- 3:21–5:21 — Justification by faith, illustrated by Abraham
- 6–8 — Sanctification, freedom from sin, life in the Spirit
- 9–11 — God's sovereign election and Israel
- 12–16 — Practical: living sacrifice, love, submission, and Christian unity

**Christ & significance.** Romans is the Reformation's charter for justification by faith alone (sola fide) and grace alone (sola gratia); its teaching on imputed righteousness and sovereign grace shaped Augustine, Luther, and Calvin.
summary: Paul to the Roman church, c. A.D. 57. The fullest exposition of the gospel: sin, justification by faith, sanctification, and election (1–11), then practical Christian living (12–16).

q: Summarize 1 Corinthians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written from Ephesus during his third missionary journey, c. A.D. 53–55, to the church he had founded in Corinth, answering reports and a letter of questions from the congregation.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Correcting disorder in a worldly, divided church by applying the wisdom of the cross to factions, sexual immorality, worship, spiritual gifts, and the resurrection.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — Divisions and the wisdom of the cross versus human wisdom
- 5–6 — Church discipline, lawsuits, and sexual purity
- 7–10 — Marriage, singleness, food offered to idols, and Christian liberty
- 11–14 — Worship, the Lord's Supper, and the right use of spiritual gifts (with love in ch. 13)
- 15 — The resurrection of Christ and of believers
- 16 — The collection and final greetings

**Christ & significance.** Christ crucified is God's wisdom and power (1:23–24); the chapter on the resurrection (15) anchors the gospel in Christ's bodily rising, and ch. 11 grounds Reformed teaching on the Lord's Supper.
summary: Paul from Ephesus, c. A.D. 53–55, to a divided and worldly Corinthian church. He applies the wisdom of the cross to factions, immorality, worship, gifts, and the resurrection.

q: Summarize 2 Corinthians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written from Macedonia, c. A.D. 55–56, shortly after 1 Corinthians, as he prepared for a third visit and defended his apostleship against rival "super-apostles."

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** A personal, emotional defense of Paul's apostolic ministry, commending the gospel of reconciliation and the power of God made perfect in human weakness.

**Outline.**
- 1–7 — Paul's ministry of the new covenant, comfort in affliction, and joy over the church's repentance
- 5:18–21 — The ministry of reconciliation
- 8–9 — The collection for the saints in Jerusalem and cheerful giving
- 10–13 — Defense of Paul's apostleship against false apostles, his boasting in weakness

**Christ & significance.** Christ became sin for us that we might become the righteousness of God (5:21) — a key text for imputation and substitutionary atonement; the letter models gospel ministry sustained by grace amid suffering.
summary: Paul from Macedonia, c. A.D. 55–56, defending his apostolic ministry to the Corinthians. He commends the gospel of reconciliation and God's strength perfected in weakness.

q: Summarize the book of Galatians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written to the churches of Galatia, possibly his earliest letter, c. A.D. 48–49 (or mid-50s), confronting Judaizers who demanded circumcision and law-keeping for salvation.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** A vigorous defense of justification by faith alone, apart from works of the law, and of the believer's freedom in Christ over against a false, works-based gospel.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Paul defends his apostolic authority and the one true gospel
- 2:16 — Justified by faith in Christ, not by works of the law
- 3–4 — Justification by faith argued from Abraham, the law as guardian, and adoption as sons
- 5–6 — Freedom, the fruit of the Spirit, and the law of love

**Christ & significance.** Galatians, with Romans, is a cornerstone of the Reformation's sola fide; Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law (3:13), and we are justified by faith, not works — Luther called it his "Katie von Bora."
summary: Paul to the Galatian churches, c. A.D. 48–49, against Judaizers. A fierce defense of justification by faith alone and freedom in Christ over a works-based gospel.

q: Summarize the book of Ephesians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (Pauline authorship affirmed), written as a prison epistle from Rome, c. A.D. 60–62, likely a circular letter to Ephesus and surrounding churches.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** God's eternal plan to unite all things in Christ, displayed in the salvation of sinners by grace and the creation of one new people — the church — from Jew and Gentile.

**Outline.**
- 1–3 — The believer's riches in Christ: election, redemption, salvation by grace through faith, and the unity of the church
- 2:8–9 — Saved by grace through faith, not works
- 4–6 — The walk that follows: unity, holiness, household relationships, and the armor of God

**Christ & significance.** Ephesians is a high point of sola gratia: salvation is wholly God's gift, not earned (2:8–9); it grounds Reformed teaching on election, union with Christ, and the church as Christ's body and bride.
summary: Paul, a prison epistle from Rome c. A.D. 60–62. God's plan to unite all things in Christ: salvation by grace through faith and the church as one new people.

q: Summarize the book of Philippians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written as a prison epistle from Rome, c. A.D. 60–62, to the beloved church at Philippi (his first European congregation), thanking them for a gift and encouraging them amid opposition.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Joy in Christ regardless of circumstances, with a call to gospel partnership, humility, and unity modeled on Christ's self-emptying.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Paul's imprisonment advancing the gospel; to live is Christ
- 2 — The humility of Christ (the kenosis hymn) as the pattern for unity
- 3 — Righteousness through faith in Christ, not works, and pressing toward the goal
- 4 — Rejoicing, contentment, peace, and thanksgiving for the gift

**Christ & significance.** The Christ-hymn (2:5–11) is a profound statement of Christ's incarnation, humiliation, and exaltation; ch. 3 reaffirms a righteousness from God that depends on faith, not on the law.
summary: Paul, a prison epistle from Rome c. A.D. 60–62, to the Philippians. A letter of joy and gospel partnership, with Christ's humility (2:5–11) as the model for unity.

q: Summarize the book of Colossians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (Pauline authorship affirmed), written as a prison epistle from Rome, c. A.D. 60–62, to the church at Colossae to counter an early syncretistic heresy diminishing Christ.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** The supremacy and all-sufficiency of Christ, in whom the fullness of deity dwells, against any philosophy or legalism that would add to him.

**Outline.**
- 1 — The preeminence of Christ, image of God and head of the church (the Christ-hymn, 1:15–20)
- 2 — Fullness in Christ alone; warning against empty philosophy, legalism, and asceticism
- 3:1–4:6 — The new life in Christ: putting off sin, putting on holiness, household duties
- 4:7–18 — Final greetings and instructions

**Christ & significance.** Colossians is a towering Christology: in Christ all the fullness of God dwells bodily (2:9), and believers are complete in him — guarding the sufficiency of Christ against every addition to the gospel.
summary: Paul, a prison epistle from Rome c. A.D. 60–62, to Colossae against a Christ-diminishing heresy. It exalts the supremacy and all-sufficiency of Christ.

q: Summarize 1 Thessalonians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (with Silas and Timothy), written from Corinth on his second missionary journey, c. A.D. 50–51, to the young church at Thessalonica soon after its founding — among his earliest letters.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Encouragement to a persecuted new church to stand firm, grow in holiness, and live in hope of Christ's return.

**Outline.**
- 1–3 — Thanksgiving for their faith and Paul's pastoral care and longing for them
- 4:1–12 — A call to sexual purity and brotherly love
- 4:13–18 — The hope of the resurrection and Christ's return
- 5 — Watchfulness for the day of the Lord and final exhortations

**Christ & significance.** A foundational text on Christ's second coming and the resurrection of believers (4:13–18); it models gospel ministry and the assurance that the dead in Christ will rise.
summary: Paul (with Silas and Timothy) from Corinth, c. A.D. 50–51, to a young persecuted church. He encourages holiness and the hope of Christ's return.

q: Summarize 2 Thessalonians: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (with Silas and Timothy), written from Corinth shortly after 1 Thessalonians, c. A.D. 51–52, to correct confusion about the day of the Lord and rebuke idleness.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Clarifying end-times teaching — the day of the Lord has not yet come — and urging steadfastness and disciplined work amid persecution.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Comfort under persecution and the certainty of God's righteous judgment
- 2 — The day of the Lord and the man of lawlessness who must come first
- 3:1–5 — A request for prayer and confidence in the Lord
- 3:6–18 — A warning against idleness and a call to disciplined work

**Christ & significance.** It guards sound eschatology against panic and error, affirming Christ's certain return in judgment and salvation, and grounds Christian hope in God's faithfulness while calling for orderly, productive living.
summary: Paul (with Silas and Timothy) from Corinth, c. A.D. 51–52. He corrects confusion about the day of the Lord and rebukes idleness, urging steadfastness.

q: Summarize 1 Timothy: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (Pauline authorship of the Pastorals affirmed), written c. A.D. 62–64 after his first Roman imprisonment, to Timothy at Ephesus to guide him in ordering the church.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Sound doctrine and good order in the household of God — qualifications for office, true worship, and faithful pastoral leadership against false teachers.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Charge to oppose false teaching; the gospel and grace shown to Paul
- 2 — Instructions on prayer and order in public worship
- 3 — Qualifications for elders (overseers) and deacons
- 4 — Guarding against apostasy; Timothy's personal godliness
- 5–6 — Care for widows, elders, slaves, contentment, and the love of money

**Christ & significance.** A key source for the Reformed doctrine of church office and ordination; it affirms Christ Jesus as the one mediator who came to save sinners (1:15; 2:5), grounding ministry in the gospel of grace.
summary: Paul to Timothy at Ephesus, c. A.D. 62–64. A Pastoral Epistle on sound doctrine, church order, and qualifications for office against false teachers.

q: Summarize 2 Timothy: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (Pauline authorship affirmed), written as a prison epistle from Rome during his second imprisonment, c. A.D. 64–67, shortly before his martyrdom — his last letter.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** A dying apostle's charge to Timothy to guard the gospel, endure suffering, and faithfully preach the inspired Word in a time of apostasy.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Encouragement to fan into flame his gift and not be ashamed of the gospel
- 2 — Endure hardship as a good soldier; rightly handle the word of truth
- 3 — The dangers of the last days and the sufficiency of inspired Scripture
- 4 — The solemn charge to preach the word; Paul's farewell, having finished the race

**Christ & significance.** The classic text on the inspiration and sufficiency of Scripture (3:16–17), foundational for the Reformed doctrine of sola Scriptura; it models faithful ministry sustained by grace to the end.
summary: Paul, a prison epistle from Rome c. A.D. 64–67, his final letter before martyrdom. He charges Timothy to guard the gospel, endure suffering, and preach the inspired Word.

q: Summarize the book of Titus: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul (Pauline authorship of the Pastorals affirmed), written c. A.D. 62–64 after his first Roman imprisonment, to Titus, whom he had left on Crete to organize the churches there.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** Ordering the church on Crete by appointing qualified elders, teaching sound doctrine, and showing that grace trains believers for good works and godly living.

**Outline.**
- 1 — Appointing qualified elders and silencing false teachers
- 2 — Sound teaching for various groups in the church
- 2:11–14 — Grace that trains us to live godly lives
- 3 — The basis of salvation in God's mercy and a call to good works and unity

**Christ & significance.** Titus 3:5 grounds salvation not in works of righteousness but in God's mercy through regeneration by the Spirit — a clear statement of sola gratia; it joins office, doctrine, and the grace-driven Christian life.
summary: Paul to Titus on Crete, c. A.D. 62–64. A Pastoral Epistle on appointing elders, sound doctrine, and grace that trains believers for godly living.

q: Summarize the book of Philemon: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Paul, written as a prison epistle from Rome, c. A.D. 60–62, a personal letter to Philemon, a Colossian believer, on behalf of his runaway slave Onesimus, now converted.

**Genre.** Epistle.

**Theme.** A tender appeal for Philemon to receive the converted Onesimus back not as a slave but as a beloved brother, illustrating gospel reconciliation and forgiveness.

**Outline.**
- 1–7 — Greeting and thanksgiving for Philemon's love and faith
- 8–16 — The appeal to welcome Onesimus as a brother in Christ
- 17–25 — Paul offers to pay any debt; final requests and greetings

**Christ & significance.** Paul's offer to bear Onesimus's debt ("charge it to me," v. 18) pictures Christ's substitutionary work, and the letter shows how the gospel transforms relationships and dissolves social barriers in Christ.
summary: Paul, a prison epistle from Rome c. A.D. 60–62, to Philemon on behalf of the converted runaway slave Onesimus. A model of gospel reconciliation and forgiveness.
